disclosure_items.clinical_fact: The pain is a burning quality and is dramatically out of proportion to the healing minor fracture; even light touch of a bedsheet is agonising.
trigger_question_examples: How bad is it compared to before?
disclosure_items.patient_response_template: It's like my foot is on fire. Even the sheet resting on it is unbearable — that's never happened before, it's way worse than when I actually broke the bone.

disclosure_items.clinical_fact: Even light mechanical contact (bedsheet, clothing, light touch) provokes severe pain in the foot and lower leg.
trigger_question_examples: Does touching the skin make it worse?
Can you wear a sock or shoe?
disclosure_items.patient_response_template: I can't have anything touch it. A sock, a shoe, even a light sheet — it all sends me into agony.

disclosure_items.clinical_fact: There is unilateral swelling and colour/temperature/sweating change in the affected limb compared with the other leg.
trigger_question_examples: Does the leg look or feel different from the other one?
Any change in colour, swelling or sweating?
disclosure_items.patient_response_template: Yes — it's puffy and the skin looks shiny and mottled, sort of red, and it seems to sweat differently to my good leg.

disclosure_items.clinical_fact: Symptoms began weeks ago after a minor tibial fracture and have progressively worsened, disproportionate to the injury.
trigger_question_examples: Was there any injury before it began?
disclosure_items.patient_response_template: It started a few weeks ago after I broke a small bone in my shin. It's been getting worse instead of better, and tonight it exploded.

disclosure_items.clinical_fact: The whole limb is worse when dependent (hanging down), producing throbbing.
trigger_question_examples: Is it worse when your leg hangs down versus raised?
Does propping the leg up help at all?
disclosure_items.patient_response_template: When my leg is down it throbs terribly. Keeping it up is the only thing that takes the edge off, a little.

disclosure_items.clinical_fact: Maximum-dose paracetamol and ibuprofen have produced no analgesic effect.
trigger_question_examples: What have you tried for the pain and did it help?
Have the paracetamol or ibuprofen made any difference?
disclosure_items.patient_response_template: I've taken as much paracetamol and ibuprofen as I'm allowed and it's done absolutely nothing.

disclosure_items.clinical_fact: The limb is not pale, cold or pulseless, and the patient has not reported the leg going white/numb — but this must be actively screened for as a limb-threatening ischaemia red flag.
trigger_question_examples: Has the foot gone pale, white, cold or numb at any point?
Can you still feel and move your toes?
disclosure_items.patient_response_template: No, it hasn't gone white or cold and I can feel my toes — it's just this burning pain and swelling.

disclosure_items.clinical_fact: No fever, and no rapidly spreading redness or red streaks tracking up the limb reported — must be screened for as an infective red flag.
trigger_question_examples: Have you had any fevers or chills?
Is the redness spreading quickly or are there red streaks moving up the leg?
disclosure_items.patient_response_template: No fevers or chills, and the redness isn't spreading in streaks — it's just the mottled colour on the foot and lower leg.

disclosure_items.clinical_fact: Lives alone with high fall risk; cluttered home with trip hazards on the path to the bathroom, and severe pain-related functional loss.
trigger_question_examples: Are you safe to move around at home tonight?
disclosure_items.patient_response_template: I live on my own. My bedroom's a bit cluttered and I'm scared of falling on the way to the bathroom because I can't use the leg.
